items.correct: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay — represented by the area below the demand curve and above the price line.
items.misconception: Consumer surplus is leftover stock that consumers haven't bought yet.
items.random_wrong: Consumer surplus is the profit that consumers make.
items.why: 'Surplus' is misleadingly physical-sounding. It is a welfare measure — the value consumers get beyond what they paid. Students take the everyday meaning of 'surplus' and miss the economic concept.

items.correct: A tax set equal to the external cost of an activity — designed to internalise the externality so the private cost reflects the social cost. Used for pollution, congestion, sugary drinks.
items.misconception: A Pigouvian tax is a tax that funds government spending.
items.random_wrong: A Pigouvian tax is a tax only on rich people.
items.why: Pigouvian taxes are corrective, not revenue-raising. The aim is to align private incentives with social welfare. Students treat all taxes as the same (revenue), missing the distinct purpose of Pigouvian intervention.

items.q: How does the sign of cross elasticity of demand (XED) reveal product relationships?
items.correct: Positive XED = substitutes (price of one rises, demand for the other rises); negative XED = complements (price of one rises, demand for the other falls); zero XED = unrelated goods.
items.misconception: The sign of XED doesn't matter — only the size does.
items.random_wrong: XED measures how often consumers cross-shop between stores.
items.why: The sign carries the qualitative information about the relationship — substitute vs complement. Size tells you the strength of the relationship. Students focus on magnitude and miss the categorical meaning of the sign.

items.q: A coal miner can't find work after pit closures because of skill mismatch. This is?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Structural. “Frictional” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Labour force = 32 million, unemployed = 1.6 million. Unemployment rate (%)?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
